descriptionLinks: Running Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform: https://docs.mat3ra.com/tutorials/ml/mattersim/
description: How to run Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform.
title: Mat3ra Tutorial: Running Python-based ML workflows in Mat3ra platform

youTubeCaptions.text: Here we will discuss how to run any Python-based machine learning workflows in Matera platform.
The easiest way to get started is by importing an existing workflow from Matera workflow bank.
If the desired workflow is not available, we can create a new workflow and select one of our available ML templates.
If no suitable template is found, we can always create a new workflow from scratch and use our general purpose Python workflow.
Now, <break time='0.25'/> let's switch to platform dot matera dot com, and see how these various options work in practice.
Option one: <break time='0.25'/> let's import an existing workflow from workflow bank that uses MatterSim machine learning model.
Navigate to the workflow bank and search for 'MatterSim'.
Before we can use this workflow to create a job, we need to copy it to our account collection.
Once copied, <break time='0.25'/> it will appear under the workflows tab.
We can open the workflow to inspect and edit it to our needs.
As we see here, this workflow consists of three units.
The first unit:  <break time='0.25'/> I O material gets the material data from the job context.
The result is an array of materials data.
The second unit: <break time='0.25'/> takes the first item of the data array and assign to another variable called 'material'.
Finally, the MatterSim unit: <break time='0.25'/> takes the 'material' variable and uses it in the MatterSim python script to predict various material properties.
Besides the input python script, the MatterSim unit also takes Python requirements file.
We can add additional python packages to the requirements file if needed by your python script.
Once happy with the changes, save and exit the workflow.
Next, navigate to the job designer page, and click create new job <break time='0.25'/> let it be under the default project.
As we see here, the jobs page is consists of three sections: <break time='0.25'/> material, workflow and compute parameters.
For this demonstration, we will proceed with default material: silicon.
Let's rename our job. Then we can select the workflow for our job, which in this case is the 'MatterSim' workflow we just imported.
Depending on our compute needs, we may adjust various parameters such as cluster, queue, number of processors, time limit, etc.
Finally, save and exit the job designer page.
Now we are ready to submit the job for execution.
Once the job is completed, we may open the MatterSim unit to view the standard output.
All output files are listed under the Files tab. We can preview or download them for further analysis.
